trajectory_summary: The call's structural problem was identified at 03:34 and never resolved. The rep discovered the caller was not the homeowner, continued qualifying through the wrong person, hit a predictable dead end on bill collection, and exited without booking an appointment or locking a path to the actual decision-maker. The call produced no appointment, no locked callback, and no contact information for the homeowner.
outcome_dispute: Primary outcome tag is 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'no appointment set, no callback locked.' The rep's closing line ('we will stay in touch') is not a soft booking; it is an unstructured exit. There is no date, no time, no commitment from either party. This call should be tagged as a lost lead unless the 'send you a request' follow-up is tracked and actioned.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Non-homeowner routing — pivot to the actual decision-maker
primary_coaching_focus.quote: No. That's my daughter. I sold my house to her a few years ago.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Every subsequent problem on this call — unverifiable bill data, impossible bill-collection request, unstructured exit — flows from the rep's failure to pivot at this moment. The rep had the information and the caller's goodwill; the correct move was to ask for Amy's contact information and route to her directly. This is the one coaching beat that, if fixed, changes the outcome of the entire call.

key_moments.quote: I just I was wondering how much it would cost and what's the rebates on it and all that, but I never got around to doing the rest of it.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's only direct cost-and-interest signal. The caller is telling the rep exactly what motivated her inquiry: cost and rebates. The rep's response ('I can definitely get you that information') is purely procedural — it promises delivery without creating any curiosity or emotional engagement. Per the codex, this is the exact moment for an inversion: the cost question is the crack the rep enters. A suggested rephrase (not verbatim): 'That's exactly what we build — your daughter's specific numbers, not a generic estimate. That's why we need her bill.' The rep instead moved to address confirmation. The flip opportunity was available and unused.

key_moments.quote: K. I do see I do see a couple of trees left here. All of those are still there.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep is looking at satellite imagery and flags tree coverage as a potential shading concern. The caller immediately corrects this: 'The backside is full of sun, and it goes over to the left side. In the front of the house, there's full sun before sunset.' This is an enthusiasm signal — the caller has direct knowledge of the sun exposure and is actively advocating for the home's solar viability. The rep acknowledged it ('Mhmm. Okay.') and moved on without anchoring on it. This was a moment to build curiosity: the caller's own description of the sun exposure is the setup for 'then your daughter's home is a strong candidate — let's get her the actual design.'

key_moments.quote: No. That's my daughter. I sold my house to her a few years ago.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep asked the correct title-check question at 03:24. The answer revealed a non-homeowner scenario: the caller is the mother; the daughter Amy is the homeowner. The codex protocol for this scenario (adult_child_decision_maker, adapted here for the inverse — parent calling on behalf of homeowner child) is clear: the actual homeowner must be present for the appointment. The rep's correct move was to ask for Amy's contact information and either call her directly or schedule with her present. Instead, the rep continued qualifying through the mother for the next seven minutes, collecting secondhand data that cannot be verified or acted on.

key_moments.quote: I do not know. I don't even know. I don't know. I don't know.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep asked the mother for the daughter's average utility bill. The mother cannot answer — she doesn't live there and doesn't have access to the bills. The rep accepted the mother's guess ('probably $300 a month') at 05:37 and logged it as the bill anchor. This is unverifiable secondhand data being treated as confirmed qualification. The bill anchor is the foundation of the entire pitch; a guessed number from a non-homeowner is not a bill anchor.
